title_en: Marriage and Commerce in Bada'i as-Sana'i
content_en:
Al-Kasani's treatment of marriage in Bada'i as-Sana'i follows his systematic framework: he defines marriage, presents its types, conditions, pillars, and consequences with the clarity and organization that distinguish this work from other Hanafi encyclopedias.

Marriage is defined by al-Kasani as 'a contract (aqd) that establishes the right of mutual enjoyment between a man and a woman for the purpose of procreation.' The essential condition of marriage, he argues, is the consent of the parties — both the groom's explicit consent and the bride's consent, whether expressed verbally or implied by the guardian contracting on her behalf with her knowledge and absence of objection.

The Hanafi position on the woman's independent contractual capacity in marriage is presented with al-Kasani's characteristic logical precision. He argues from the principle that Islamic law grants the adult woman full contractual capacity in all commercial matters; marriage, as a contract, falls within this capacity. The counter-argument that the Prophet said 'There is no marriage without a guardian' is addressed by interpreting it as establishing the guardian's right to be consulted (a shari' requirement of courtesy and propriety) rather than a condition of contractual validity.

The chapter on mahr presents the Hanafi minimum of ten dirhams with al-Kasani's defense. He argues that the minimum was established by the Companions' practice and is consistent with the principle that mahr is a material expression of the husband's honor and commitment to the wife. A mahr below the minimum would not serve this social function and is therefore replaced by the minimum in Hanafi law.

In commercial transactions, al-Kasani's treatment of the elements of a sale (bay') is among the clearest in classical fiqh. He identifies four elements: the contracting parties (al-'aqidain), the offer and acceptance (al-ijab wal-qabul), the sold object (al-ma'qud 'alayhi), and the price (ath-thaman). For each element, he specifies the conditions of validity: the contracting parties must have legal capacity; the offer and acceptance must be in the same session (majlis) without interruption; the sold object must be permissible, owned, and capable of delivery; and the price must be known.

Al-Kasani's chapter on riba is among the most analytically clear in Hanafi literature. He identifies the two types of riba and their respective causes. Riba al-fadl (excess riba) occurs when two items of the same ribawi category are exchanged in unequal amounts. Riba an-nasi'ah (delay riba) occurs when two items of the same ribawi category are exchanged with a delay. The legal cause ('illah) that triggers riba rules in the Hanafi school is 'measurement and genus' — items measured by weight or volume of the same kind. Al-Kasani's clear articulation of this 'illah allows systematic extension: any two items sharing measurement and genus are subject to riba rules when exchanged among themselves.

The work concludes the commercial law section by noting that the Shari'ah's regulation of commerce is not an imposition on human freedom but a gift of divine wisdom — establishing the conditions under which commerce produces justice rather than exploitation, mutual benefit rather than zero-sum extraction. The Muslim scholar's task in each era is to apply these timeless principles to the commercial realities of their time with wisdom, precision, and fidelity to the revealed sources.
